sections.title: Global Equities
sections.content:
Global equity markets posted their strongest monthly performance of 2026 in May, with risk appetite surging on the back of an unprecedented AI-driven earnings cycle that overwhelmed residual geopolitical headwinds from the US-Iran conflict. The MSCI World rallied broadly across Developed and Emerging Markets alike, anchored by a historic re-rating of the technology and semiconductor complex; the S&P 500 gained **5.3%** to close at a new all-time high of **7,599.96**, while the Nasdaq Composite surged **8.9%** to **27,086.81** — its best single-month advance in several years.

Developed Market ex-US indices also participated constructively, with the DAX advancing **5.0%** to **25,104.70** and Japanese equities gaining approximately **4-6%** on AI-related chip demand. Emerging Markets outperformed materially, estimated up approximately **7-9%**, underpinned by continued AI infrastructure investment and mid-month dollar softness. The month's defining contradiction was that record equity prices coexisted with Brent crude remaining well above **$100/bbl** — a testament to how powerfully AI-driven earnings momentum can override conventional macro-inflation headwinds when corporate fundamentals are sufficiently robust.

sections.title: Your Must-Know Market Themes
sections.content: Four key themes that shaped markets this month:
themes:
AI Mega-Cycle Overpowers Geopolitical Headwinds -- The singular force animating global risk appetite in May was the relentless demand for artificial intelligence infrastructure. Leading technology companies have now committed more than **$600 billion** in aggregate 2026 capital expenditure to build the AI industry's physical backbone — a level of investment with no historical precedent — and this spending translated directly into revenue acceleration across the supply chain. The S&P 500 technology sector surged approximately **20%** for the month — the strongest sectoral return in decades — as Q1 2026 earnings came in roughly **27%** above prior-year levels, more than double consensus expectations, with hyperscalers and GPU manufacturers reporting record order backlogs.

The Nasdaq Composite gained **8.9%** and the Nasdaq 100 gained **10.6%**, with NVIDIA, Broadcom, and peer semiconductor names accounting for a disproportionate share of index-level returns. Critically, this rally was not a pure valuation-expansion story; it was validated by hard earnings beats across the AI supply chain, cementing investor conviction that the secular AI build-out is at an early-to-mid stage with no near-term demand saturation. US stocks posted their best May performance since 1990, underscoring the historic magnitude of this risk appetite surge.

US-Iran Conflict -- Oil Shock Peaks, Peace Signals Emerge -- The residual tail risk from the February 28 US-Israel military operation against Iran continued to exert upward pressure on energy prices throughout May, though the month marked a pivotal inflection toward de-escalation. Brent crude opened the month above **$116/bbl** before declining toward approximately **$100-108/bbl** by late May as diplomatic signals emerged; on May 29, the US and Iran agreed on a preliminary memorandum of understanding extending a ceasefire for 60 days and opening permanent peace negotiations, including unrestricted passage through the Strait of Hormuz.

Despite oil's retreat from its highs, a geopolitical risk premium of meaningful magnitude persisted, with US headline CPI reaching **4.2%** in May — the highest reading since April 2023 — with energy prices up more than **23%** year-on-year. OPEC pledged incremental production increases widely characterised as symbolic, while the UAE's effective departure from the cartel underscored the bloc's diminishing cohesion. Central banks found it increasingly difficult to credibly look through energy-driven cost-push inflation, raising the spectre of a policy overtightening scenario.

Fed Transition -- Warsh Era Begins with a Hawkish Hold -- On May 15, Kevin Warsh was sworn in as the 17th Chair of the Federal Reserve, succeeding Jerome Powell after eight years. In his first FOMC meeting, the Committee voted unanimously to hold the federal funds rate at **3.50%-3.75%**, but the accompanying communications represented a clear hawkish pivot. The FOMC revised its core PCE inflation forecast sharply higher to **3.6%** for 2026 (from **2.7%**), acknowledging that oil-driven cost-push inflation is proving stickier than the look-through framework had anticipated.

Futures markets moved to price greater than **50%** probability of a Fed rate hike at the December 2026 meeting — a stark re-pricing from near-zero hike probability at the start of the month. The 30-year Treasury yield spiked above **5.00%** intra-month following back-to-back hot inflation prints — levels last seen in 2007 — forcing a re-rating of duration-sensitive assets and providing tailwinds to the dollar's mid-month recovery. The updated median dot plot implies the bar for cuts has risen materially, upending consensus easing assumptions that had anchored rate-sensitive sector pricing since late 2025.

Record Q1 Earnings Validate the Rally's Foundation -- The Q1 2026 earnings season, which concluded in May, delivered the most decisive upside surprise since the post-pandemic recovery cycle. S&P 500 earnings are on pace for approximately **27%** year-over-year growth — more than double the **12%** consensus entering the season — driven by AI-related revenue acceleration in technology, resilient consumer spending in discretionary, and strong energy-sector cash flows. Megacap names including NVIDIA, Alphabet, and Amazon all reported results that materially exceeded the highest buy-side estimates, prompting a series of forward guidance upgrades.

This earnings momentum provided the fundamental underpinning distinguishing May's rally from purely sentiment-driven rebounds; the S&P 500's forward P/E multiple expanded only modestly, suggesting the market is broadly paying for genuine earnings power rather than multiple expansion alone. The **$600 billion+** in committed AI capital expenditure is forecasted to drive continued revenue upside in subsequent quarters, sustaining the bull case for technology-sector re-rating into the second half of 2026. Aggregate US corporate earnings are now tracking materially ahead of pre-year forecasts, raising full-year estimates across the street and cementing the view that the AI-driven productivity cycle is real and durable.

sections.title: US Equities
sections.content:
The US equity market delivered a historically strong May, with the S&P 500 gaining **5.3%** to close at a new all-time high of **7,599.96** and the Nasdaq Composite rising **8.9%** to **27,086.81** — its best single-month advance since the AI re-rating of 2023. The Dow Jones Industrial Average crossed the **51,000** milestone for the first time in history, settling at **51,078.88**. US stocks recorded their best May performance since 1990, with the S&P 500's nine consecutive weekly gains — a streak observed only four other times in forty years — reflecting an unusual convergence of robust corporate fundamentals, AI-driven sentiment, and easing geopolitical pessimism.

The rally was characterised by extreme concentration in the technology and semiconductor complex: the S&P 500 tech sector surged approximately **20%** in aggregate, accounting for all ten of the index's top individual stock contributors for the month. The Nasdaq 100 advanced **10.6%**, with NVIDIA, Broadcom, and peer semiconductor names responsible for a disproportionate share of index-level returns. Breadth, while improving from March's lows, remained skewed toward large-cap growth, with the Russell 2000 modestly underperforming as higher-for-longer rate expectations weighed on small-cap refinancing costs.

Energy's earlier YTD leadership was definitively eclipsed by technology's total return by month-end, marking a decisive regime change in sector-level return leadership for 2026. The Q1 earnings season concluded with approximately **27%** year-over-year EPS growth against a **12%** consensus — the most decisive upside surprise since the post-pandemic recovery cycle — providing fundamental validation that distinguished this advance from a purely liquidity-driven re-rating.

sections.title: European Equities
sections.content:
European equities delivered a volatile but ultimately positive month, with the DAX advancing **5.0%** to close at **25,104.70** while the CAC 40 and FTSE 100 posted more modest recoveries from mid-month weakness. The CAC 40 ended near approximately **8,251** (approx., verify before merging), a gain of approximately **3.4%**, while the FTSE 100 finished near approximately **8,200**. The principal headwinds were the twin pressures of elevated energy costs — Brent crude remaining above **$100/bbl** throughout the month — and renewed UK political uncertainty, where Prime Minister Starmer's government faced increasing parliamentary pressure.

A sharp mid-month selloff saw FTSE 100 futures fall approximately **1.7%** and continental indices retreat over **1%** on May 18, driven by fears that Iranian peace talks would stall; the subsequent ceasefire extension announcement on May 29 provided a meaningful late-month recovery that preserved positive monthly returns for most European markets. European corporate earnings were bifurcated: energy names (bp, TotalEnergies, Shell) continued to benefit from elevated hydrocarbon prices, while industrial conglomerates and auto manufacturers flagged material margin pressure from elevated input costs.

The Stoxx 600 defence sub-index remained a notable outperformer as European NATO members accelerated rearmament spending commitments in response to the Middle East conflict, extending the multi-month trend of defence-sector re-rating. The Bank of England maintained a cautious tone on rate normalisation given elevated imported inflation, providing limited near-term support to UK equity valuations relative to continental peers.

sections.title: APAC Equities
sections.content:
Asia-Pacific equities posted solid gains in May, led by Japan and the broader technology-linked EM segment, though the region's performance was bifurcated between AI supply-chain beneficiaries and markets weighed by domestic headwinds. Japanese equities advanced approximately **4-6%** for the month, with the Nikkei 225 extending its year-to-date gain to approximately **22.5%** as semiconductor and electronics exporters benefited directly from surging global AI capital expenditure demand. South Korean and Taiwanese indices similarly outperformed, though management teams at TSMC, Foxconn, and Infineon flagged supply-chain disruptions and logistical challenges stemming from Strait of Hormuz tensions as an emerging operational risk.

The Hang Seng Index posted a marginal monthly loss of approximately **-1.0%**, weighed down by ongoing property-sector overhang and a geopolitical risk premium on Hong Kong-listed Chinese technology names. The Shanghai Composite declined approximately **-2.0%** through mid-month before a late-month recovery on ceasefire signals, with the final monthly reading partially offset by targeted fiscal stimulus from Beijing's Ministry of Finance and resilient domestic consumption data.

Across the region, the overriding institutional narrative was AI-led re-engagement by global capital, even as supply-chain complexity from the Middle East situation added operational uncertainty. The MSCI AC Asia Pacific index advanced an estimated **5-8%** for the month, supported by the dual tailwinds of AI infrastructure demand and mid-month dollar softness that provided a tailwind to EM returns denominated in local currencies.

sections.title: Foreign Exchange
sections.content:
The US Dollar Index (DXY) recovered to approximately **99-100** through May, as the hawkish Warsh-era Fed repricing and the dollar's residual safe-haven bid from Middle East uncertainty outweighed the mid-month peace-deal optimism that had briefly pressured the greenback. US headline CPI reaching **4.2%** — the highest reading since April 2023, with energy prices up more than **23%** year-on-year — reinforced the higher-for-longer rate narrative that sustained dollar demand through the month. J.P. Morgan Global Research upgraded its dollar outlook in mid-May, citing the macro shift in Fed sentiment and signs of labor market resilience, providing a further medium-term constructive signal for the greenback.

EUR/USD ended May near **1.165**, broadly range-bound as European growth concerns and persistent energy inflation in the Eurozone offset any sustained dollar weakness; the pair's inability to sustain a push above **1.17** reflects the durable headwind of higher US rates for longer. GBP/USD closed near **1.343**, pulling back from an early-May push toward the mid-1.36s as UK political uncertainty resurfaced and the Bank of England maintained a cautious tone on rate normalisation.

USD/JPY traded in a wide range during the month, settling near **154-156** at month-end; the Bank of Japan's gradual policy normalisation continued to provide yen support on dips, though the persistent US-Japan rate differential capped the magnitude of any yen appreciation. Commodity-linked currencies (AUD, CAD) tracked oil's declining trajectory through mid-month before recovering on peace-deal signals, ending broadly flat on a DXY-adjusted basis.

sections.title: Commodities
sections.content:
The commodities complex in May was defined by a partial rolling back of the extreme war-risk premium that had characterised March and April, though prices remained structurally elevated relative to pre-conflict levels. Brent crude declined from approximately **$116/bbl** at the month's open toward approximately **$100-108/bbl** at month-end as Strait of Hormuz tensions eased on ceasefire signals; WTI tracked approximately **$3-5/bbl** below Brent (approx., verify before merging), consistent with its historical discount, and similarly trended lower through the month. Despite the retreat, both benchmarks remained well above pre-conflict levels, sustaining a geopolitical risk premium that central banks could not credibly look through indefinitely.

Gold consolidated after its earlier 2026 all-time high of approximately **$5,589/oz**, trading in the **$4,700-4,750/oz** range through May — a correction of **15%** from the peak — as dollar strength and rising real yields compressed safe-haven demand; J.P. Morgan's end-year target of **$6,000/oz** nonetheless signals continued institutional conviction in the gold bull case.

Copper was the standout outperformer across the metals complex, advancing approximately **6.5%** month-over-month to near **$6.00/lb**, driven by AI data-centre infrastructure demand for electrical components and a structural supply deficit that the market has been pricing since early 2025. The CME Group's May 2026 micro metals update confirmed copper's outperformance relative to gold and silver, reflecting the metal's unique exposure to the AI-driven build-out of physical computing infrastructure and data-centre wiring requirements.

sections.title: Fixed Income
sections.content:
US Treasuries experienced a pronounced bear-steepening dynamic in May as the incoming Warsh FOMC repriced the terminal rate higher while the long end absorbed the dual pressures of fiscal expansion and elevated inflation expectations. The 10-year Treasury yield settled at **4.48%** at month-end, while the 30-year yield spiked above **5.00%** intra-month following back-to-back hot CPI and PPI prints — levels last seen in 2007. The 2-year Treasury yield closed near **4.13%**, reflecting the market's repricing of a meaningful probability of at least one additional rate hike before year-end; the 2s10s curve held a positive slope of approximately **+35 basis points**, a normalised configuration following the prolonged inversion of 2023-2024.

The FOMC's upward revision of core PCE inflation to **3.6%** for 2026 — up sharply from **2.7%** — was the central fixed-income story of the month, mechanically extending the duration of the higher-for-longer regime and compressing expectations for near-term easing. Futures markets moved to price greater than **50%** probability of a rate hike at the December 2026 FOMC meeting — a stark re-pricing from near-zero probability at the start of 2026 — forcing a re-rating of duration-sensitive assets across the rate spectrum. The Fed funds rate was held at **3.50%-3.75%** for the third consecutive meeting, but the updated median dot plot implies the bar for cuts has risen materially.

Credit markets remained well-supported despite the rate volatility: investment-grade spreads were broadly stable and high-yield spreads tightened modestly as the equity rally and robust earnings bolstered issuer creditworthiness. New issuance was well-absorbed across the credit spectrum, with the US Aggregate Bond Index returning approximately **+0.3%** for the month as corporate bond outperformance offset duration drag from rising Treasury yields.
